days.grammar: Past tense of 'to be'; affirmative, negative, questions
days.notes: The past tense of 'to be' uses 'was' for I/he/she/it and 'were' for you/we/they. Forms: I was, you were, he/she/it was, we were, they were. Note that 'you' takes 'were' even in singular (in modern English; archaic 'thou wast' is not used). Negation: I was not/wasn't, you weren't, he/she/it wasn't, we weren't, they weren't. Questions: Was I...?, Were you...?, Was he...?, Were we...?, Were they...? Short answers: 'Yes, I was' or 'No, I wasn't.' Past simple of 'to be' is used to describe states in the past: 'I was happy,' 'She was at home,' 'They were students,' 'The weather was cold.' Common uses: (1) Location: 'Where were you yesterday?' 'I was at home.' (2) State/Emotion: 'How was your day?' 'It was great.' 'I was tired,' 'She was nervous.' (3) Age: 'How old were you in 2010?' 'I was 15.' (4) Time reference: 'When was your birthday?' 'It was last month.' Spanish speakers must understand that 'was/were' is only for 'to be' in past; it is NOT used as an auxiliary with regular verbs (that's Week 8 Day 4 with 'did'). Also, notice that there are only TWO forms (was/were), not multiple conjugations like Spanish 'era, eras, era, éramos, erais, eran.' This makes English past simple of 'to be' simpler than Spanish, though Spanish speakers often confuse it with other past tense forms.

examples.en: I was at home yesterday.
examples.es: Estaba en casa ayer.

examples.en: She was happy. / She wasn't happy.
examples.es: Ella estaba feliz. / Ella no estaba feliz.

examples.en: Were you in London last year? Yes, I was.
examples.es: ¿Estuviste en Londres el año pasado? Sí.

examples.en: The weather was cold and rainy.
examples.es: El clima era frío y lluvioso.

examples.en: How old were you in 2010? I was 12.
examples.es: ¿Cuántos años tenías en 2010? Tenía 12.

examples.en: They weren't at the party. Where were they?
examples.es: No estaban en la fiesta. ¿Dónde estaban?

days.tip: PAST SIMPLE 'TO BE': I/he/she/it WAS (singular), you/we/they WERE (plural). Questions: Was I/he/she/it...? Were you/we/they...? Short answers with 'was/were.' This is ONLY for 'to be' — don't use 'was/were' with other verbs. Spanish speakers must distinguish: 'was/were' (past of 'to be') vs 'did' (auxiliary for regular verbs in past — coming Day 4).

practice.answer: I was happy
Where were you yesterday?
She wasn't at home
How old were you in 2015?
Yes, they were.

flashcards.front: Yes, I was / No, I wasn't

quiz.q: What does 'You were in London' describe?

days.grammar: Regular verb formation with -ed; pronunciation of -ed endings
days.notes: The past simple of regular verbs is formed by adding -ed to the base verb. All subjects use the same form: I worked, you worked, he/she/it worked, we worked, they worked. Spelling rules: (1) Most verbs add -ed: work → worked, play → played, walk → walked, talk → talked, watch → watched. (2) Verbs ending in -e add just -d: love → loved, move → moved, like → liked. (3) Verbs ending in consonant + -y change -y to -ies: study → studied, carry → carried, try → tried. (4) Verbs ending in vowel + -y just add -ed: play → played, enjoy → enjoyed. (5) One-syllable verbs with consonant + vowel + consonant, double the consonant: stop → stopped, plan → planned, prefer → preferred. Pronunciation of -ed has three forms: /t/ after voiceless sounds (worked, walked, watched), /d/ after voiced sounds (played, moved, loved), /ɪd/ after -t or -d sounds (wanted, needed). Time expressions used with past simple: yesterday, last week, ago, in 2020, at 3 o'clock, on Monday, etc. Past simple regular verbs are used for completed actions in the past: 'I studied English last night,' 'She worked yesterday,' 'They walked in the park.' Spanish speakers need to understand that English regular past (-ed) is much simpler than Spanish past tenses. Spanish has preterite (regular conjugations with -é, -aste, etc.) and imperfect (different endings). English uses -ed for ALL regular verbs regardless of subject. This makes English regular past MUCH easier than Spanish.

examples.en: I worked in a bank last year.
examples.es: Trabajé en un banco el año pasado.

examples.en: She studied English yesterday.
examples.es: Ella estudió inglés ayer.

examples.en: They walked in the park last Sunday.
examples.es: Ellos caminaron en el parque el domingo pasado.

examples.en: He wanted to go but he stayed home.
examples.es: Él quería ir pero se quedó en casa.

examples.en: We played football last week.
examples.es: Jugamos fútbol la semana pasada.

examples.en: I loved that film!
examples.es: ¡Me encantó esa película!

days.tip: REGULAR PAST SIMPLE: Base verb + -ed (all subjects same). 'Worked' (NOT 'work'), 'studied' (NOT 'study'). Spelling: most add -ed, drop -e before -d (love→loved), -y→-ied (study→studied). This is MUCH simpler than Spanish conjugations! One form for everyone. Time expressions mark past: 'yesterday,' 'last week,' '2 hours ago.' Pronunciation: /t/ (worked), /d/ (played), /ɪd/ (wanted) — doesn't affect spelling.

practice.answer: worked, loved, studied, stopped, planned
I played football yesterday
She didn't study last night (Day 4 structure)
When did you finish work? (uses 'did' + base form, Day 4)
I walked last week / I walked a week ago

days.grammar: Irregular verb forms; memorization required; usage context
days.notes: English has many irregular verbs that do NOT follow the -ed pattern. These must be memorized. Common irregular verbs at A1 level: go→went, eat→ate, drink→drank, have→had, make→made, see→saw, come→came, take→took, buy→bought, give→gave, say→said, tell→told, get→got, think→thought, know→knew, find→found, leave→left, begin→began, start→started (wait, that's regular), become→became, feel→felt. The pattern varies: some have vowel changes (go/went, eat/ate, drink/drank, take/took, swim/swam), some add -t (buy/bought, think/thought, catch/caught), some are completely different (go/went, be/was-were). These must be learned through repetition and use. All subjects use the same form in past simple: I went, you went, he went, etc. (unlike Spanish where ir/ir conjugates as fui/fuiste/fue/fuimos/fuisteis/fueron). Irregular verbs are used in the same way as regular verbs but with different base forms. For negation and questions, you use 'did' + base form, even for irregular verbs: 'I didn't go,' 'Did he go?', 'I went' (affirmative). The key point for Spanish speakers is that English irregular verbs are relatively few compared to Spanish, and once you memorize them, you can use them for all subjects without conjugation changes.

examples.en: I went to Madrid last week.
examples.es: Fui a Madrid la semana pasada.

examples.en: She ate pizza for dinner.
examples.es: Ella comió pizza para cenar.

examples.en: They came to my house yesterday.
examples.es: Ellos vinieron a mi casa ayer.

examples.en: He took a taxi to the station.
examples.es: Él tomó un taxi a la estación.

examples.en: I thought about you. Did you think about me?
examples.es: Pensé en ti. ¿Pensaste en mí?

examples.en: They saw a beautiful film. They said it was excellent.
examples.es: Vieron una película hermosa. Dijeron que fue excelente.

days.tip: IRREGULAR VERBS — NO PATTERN: go/went, eat/ate, drink/drank, etc. MUST MEMORIZE. All subjects the same: 'I went, you went, he went' (no changes). For negation/questions: 'I didn't go' (base form after 'didn't'), 'Did you go?' — see Week 8 Day 4. This is one-time memorization work, but worth it because these are the most common verbs. Focus on high-frequency ones first: go, have, say, make, come, see, take, think, know.

practice.prompt: Give past form: go, eat, have, see, come
practice.answer: went, ate, had, saw, came
saw
went

practice.prompt: He ___ (take) a photo of the sunset
practice.answer: He took a photo of the sunset

practice.prompt: Write 4 sentences using different irregular verbs in past
practice.answer: I went to the store. I saw my friend. We took a walk. We had coffee.

days.grammar: Negation with didn't + base verb; questions with did + base verb; short answers
days.notes: The negation of past simple uses 'didn't' (did + not) + base verb for ALL verbs (regular and irregular). Key rule: the main verb is ALWAYS in base form, never past form. WRONG: 'I didn't went,' 'She didn't worked.' CORRECT: 'I didn't go,' 'She didn't work.' This applies to both regular and irregular verbs. Examples: 'I didn't work yesterday,' 'She didn't study,' 'He didn't go,' 'They didn't see.' Questions use 'did' + subject + base verb: 'Did I work?' 'Did she study?' 'Did he go?' 'Did they see?' Again, the base verb is used, not the past form. WRONG: 'Did you went?', 'Did he studied?' CORRECT: 'Did you go?', 'Did he study?' Short answers use only the auxiliary: 'Did you work?' 'Yes, I did' or 'No, I didn't.' You don't repeat the verb in short answers. Time expressions: 'yesterday,' 'last week,' '3 days ago,' 'in 2020.' Spanish speakers often struggle with this because in Spanish you conjugate the main verb: 'No fui' (did not go), but in English you must use 'did' + base form. This is a critical difference. Also, note that 'did' is the ONLY auxiliary used for all past simple questions and negatives — there's no variation.

examples.en: Did you work yesterday? Yes, I did. / No, I didn't.
examples.es: ¿Trabajaste ayer? Sí. / No.

examples.en: She didn't go to the party. Why didn't she go?
examples.es: Ella no fue a la fiesta. ¿Por qué no fue?

examples.en: I didn't see him last week.
examples.es: No lo vi la semana pasada.

examples.en: Did they eat breakfast? No, they didn't. They didn't have time.
examples.es: ¿Comieron desayuno? No. No tuvieron tiempo.

examples.en: He didn't take the bus. He took a taxi.
examples.es: Él no tomó el autobús. Tomó un taxi.

examples.en: Didn't you study? Yes, I did study, but I didn't understand.
examples.es: ¿No estudiaste? Sí estudié, pero no entendí.

days.tip: PAST SIMPLE NEGATION & QUESTIONS — CRITICAL RULE: 'Did' + BASE VERB (never past form). 'I didn't go' (NOT 'didn't went'), 'Did you see?' (NOT 'Did you saw?'). This applies to BOTH regular (worked) and irregular (went) verbs. Short answer: 'Yes, I did' (NOT 'Yes, I went'). This rule is VERY DIFFERENT from Spanish and is a major point of confusion. Practice this until it becomes automatic.

practice.answer: I didn't work yesterday
Did she see the film?

practice.prompt: He went to the park (make negative)
practice.answer: He didn't go to the park
No, I didn't
Did you study? Yes, I did study math. But I didn't study English.

flashcards.back: Did not + base form (went is past, use 'go')

flashcards.front: Yes, I did / No, I didn't
Did he see? / He didn't see

days.grammar: Past simple all forms: to be, regular verbs, irregular verbs, negation, questions
days.notes: Week 8 has been intense because you've learned an entire new tense — the past simple, which is essential for talking about completed actions in the past. You've covered: (1) Past simple of 'to be' (was/were), (2) Regular verbs with -ed, (3) Irregular verbs (memorized forms), (4) Negation with didn't + base verb, (5) Questions with did + base verb. The past simple is used for actions completed at a specific time in the past: 'I worked yesterday,' 'She went to Madrid last week,' 'They ate pizza.' Spanish speakers must remember that English past simple is much simpler than Spanish past tenses — there's only ONE simple past form per verb (no preterite vs imperfect distinction), and questions/negatives use 'did' as an auxiliary, leaving the main verb in base form. This is a critical difference from Spanish where you conjugate the main verb. By mastering Week 8, you now can talk about your past, ask about others' past actions, and describe what happened. Week 9 introduces 'going to' for future plans, and Week 10 will introduce adjectives for comparisons (comparative/superlative).

examples.en: Yesterday was a great day! I was happy all day. I worked in the morning. Then I went to lunch with my friend Sarah. We ate at a wonderful restaurant. The food was delicious! After lunch, we walked in the park and saw some beautiful birds. We talked for hours. At 6 PM I came home and had dinner with my family. It was a perfect day!
examples.es: ¡Ayer fue un gran día! Estuve feliz todo el día. Trabajé por la mañana. Luego fui a almorzar con mi amiga Sarah. Comimos en un restaurante maravilloso. ¡La comida fue deliciosa! Después del almuerzo, caminamos en el parque y vimos algunos pájaros hermosos. Hablamos durante horas. A las 6 PM volví a casa y cené con mi familia. ¡Fue un día perfecto!

examples.en: Did you have a good weekend? Yes, I did. Where did you go? I went to the beach. Did you swim? No, I didn't swim, but I played football. Did you like it? Yes, I loved it! The weather was beautiful and I saw many friends. We had a picnic and ate sandwiches and fruit. It was wonderful!
examples.es: ¿Tuviste un buen fin de semana? Sí. ¿Dónde fuiste? Fui a la playa. ¿Nadaste? No, no nadé, pero jugué fútbol. ¿Te gustó? ¡Sí, me encantó! El clima fue hermoso y vi a muchos amigos. Hicimos un picnic y comimos sándwiches y fruta. ¡Fue maravilloso!

examples.en: Last year I studied very hard. I didn't go out much. I focused on my work. I went to the library every day. I read many books and took many notes. I didn't watch TV. I didn't play games. I was serious about learning. This year I want to be more balanced. I want to work hard but also have fun.
examples.es: El año pasado estudié mucho. No salí mucho. Me enfoqué en mi trabajo. Iba a la biblioteca todos los días. Leí muchos libros y tomé muchas notas. No veía TV. No jugaba juegos. Fui serio sobre aprender. Este año quiero ser más equilibrado. Quiero trabajar duro pero también divertirme.

days.tip: WEEK 8 SUMMARY: (1) 'Was/were' for 'to be' past (I was, you were, he was). (2) Regular -ed for most verbs (worked, studied). (3) Irregular verbs must be memorized (went, ate, saw, etc.). (4) Negation: didn't + BASE VERB (I didn't go, NOT 'didn't went'). (5) Questions: did + subject + BASE VERB (Did you see?, NOT 'Did you saw?'). This single tense opens up the entire past — use it constantly!

practice.instruction: Describe a day in the past using past simple
practice.prompt: Write 8-10 sentences about your day yesterday
practice.answer: I woke up at 7 AM. I was tired. I had breakfast and drank coffee. I worked from 9 to 5. I didn't take lunch. At 6 PM I came home. I watched TV. I had dinner with my family. We talked for an hour. I went to bed at 11 PM.

practice.instruction: Ask and answer past simple questions
practice.prompt: Create 5 questions about someone's week, answer them
practice.answer: Did you work? Yes, I did. Where did you go? I went to the store. Did you see your friends? No, I didn't. What did you do? I studied.

practice.prompt: Write 6 sentences using 3 regular and 3 irregular past verbs
practice.answer: I worked yesterday. I saw a film. I played football. I took a bus. I studied English. I came home at 6.

practice.prompt: Take 3 past simple statements and convert to negatives and questions
practice.answer: I went → I didn't go / Did I go? / She studied → She didn't study / Did she study? / They had lunch → They didn't have lunch / Did they have lunch?

practice.prompt: Describe a past event with feelings, actions, people involved, time
practice.answer: Last week was busy. I was stressed. I worked hard. I didn't sleep much. I saw my family. We went to the park. We talked about our plans. We felt happy. The weather was nice.

flashcards.front: Yes, I did / No, I didn't
All subjects same form in past

quiz.q: Which is ALL correct past simple?
options: I worked, she studied, he go
I worked, she studied, he went
I worked, she studied, he goes
I worked, she studying, he went

days.grammar: 'Going to' structure for future; near-future predictions
days.notes: The 'going to' structure expresses plans, intentions, and predictions for the near future. Formation: am/is/are + going to + base verb. All subjects: I am going to work, you are going to work, he/she/it is going to work, we are going to work, they are going to work. Contractions: I'm going to, you're going to, he's going to, etc. Negation: I am not going to / I'm not going to + base verb. Questions: Am/Is/Are + subject + going to + base verb? Short answers: Yes, I am / No, I'm not. Uses: (1) Future plans: 'I'm going to travel next month,' 'She's going to study medicine.' (2) Intentions: 'I'm going to quit my job,' 'We're going to move house.' (3) Near-future predictions: 'It's going to rain,' 'You're going to love this film.' (4) Scheduled/arranged plans: 'I'm going to meet my friend tomorrow,' 'They're going to have a party.' 'Going to' is similar to Spanish 'ir a + infinitivo' (voy a trabajar), so Spanish speakers should find this structure familiar. The key difference is that in English, 'going to' ALWAYS uses the base form of the verb after it, while Spanish sometimes uses different infinitive forms. Time expressions: 'tomorrow,' 'next week,' 'later,' 'this evening,' 'in two days.' These mark the future time and are essential for context. Note that 'going to' refers to the near future or specific plans, not distant/uncertain future (that would use 'will,' not taught at A1).

examples.en: I'm going to study English tomorrow.
examples.es: Voy a estudiar inglés mañana.

examples.en: She is going to travel to Spain next month.
examples.es: Ella va a viajar a España el próximo mes.

examples.en: Are you going to come to the party? Yes, I am.
examples.es: ¿Vas a venir a la fiesta? Sí.

examples.en: It's going to rain this evening.
examples.es: Va a llover esta tarde.

examples.en: What are you going to do this weekend? We're going to go to the beach.
examples.es: ¿Qué van a hacer este fin de semana? Vamos a ir a la playa.

examples.en: I'm not going to work tomorrow. I'm going to rest.
examples.es: No voy a trabajar mañana. Voy a descansar.

days.tip: 'GOING TO' = SPANISH 'IR A + INFINITIVE': Am/is/are going to + base verb. Used for plans and near-future. Contractions standard: 'I'm going to,' 'he's going to.' Questions: 'Are you going to...?' Short answers use 'am/is/are,' not the full phrase. Time words mark future: 'tomorrow,' 'next week,' 'this weekend.' This is simpler than other English future structures (like 'will') because it's closer to your current plans.

practice.answer: I'm going to study tonight / I am going to study tonight
Is she going to go shopping tomorrow? / Is she going to shop tomorrow?

practice.prompt: They are not going to come
practice.answer: They're not going to come / They aren't going to come
Yes, I am

practice.prompt: List 3 things you're going to do this week
practice.answer: I'm going to study English. I'm going to see my friends. I'm going to go shopping.

flashcards.front: Yes, I am / No, I'm not

flashcards.back: Always: going to WORK (not 'going to working')

quiz.q: Form: 'I travel to Paris next month'

days.grammar: Verb + -ing after these preference verbs; hobbies and leisure
days.notes: Certain verbs related to preferences are followed by the -ing form of a verb, not the infinitive. These verbs are: like, love, hate, enjoy, and some others. Structure: like/love/hate/enjoy + verb-ing. Examples: 'I like swimming,' 'She loves cooking,' 'He hates waking up early,' 'We enjoy reading,' 'They like playing games.' This is different from the infinitive structure with other verbs. Note: with 'like,' you can say either 'I like to swim' OR 'I like swimming' — both are correct, but -ing is more common in British English. Negative: 'I don't like swimming,' 'She doesn't love cooking.' Questions: 'Do you like swimming?' 'Does he enjoy reading?' The -ing forms here follow the same spelling rules as present continuous: work → working, make → making, run → running, etc. Common hobbies/leisure activities: swimming, reading, cooking, dancing, playing guitar, watching TV, going to the cinema, playing football, taking photos, listening to music, writing, drawing, painting, cycling. These are essential for talking about personal interests and making friends. Spanish speakers should note that Spanish often uses the infinitive after verbs like 'gustar' (me gusta nadar = I like to swim / I like swimming), but English REQUIRES the -ing form for these preference verbs. You cannot say 'I like to swim' vs 'I like swimming' interchangeably in formal grammar — both exist, but -ing is preferred.

examples.en: What do you like doing? I like swimming and playing football.
examples.es: ¿Qué te gusta hacer? Me gusta nadar y jugar fútbol.

examples.en: She loves cooking Italian food.
examples.es: A ella le encanta cocinar comida italiana.

examples.en: He hates waking up early, but he enjoys working.
examples.es: A él le odio despertarse temprano, pero le encanta trabajar.

examples.en: Do you like going to the cinema? Yes, I love it!
examples.es: ¿Te gusta ir al cine? ¡Sí, me encanta!

examples.en: They enjoy playing games in the evening.
examples.es: Ellos disfrutan jugando juegos por la noche.

examples.en: I don't like watching TV, but I like reading books.
examples.es: No me gusta ver TV, pero me gusta leer libros.

days.tip: PREFER VERB + -ING: 'I like swimming' (NOT 'I like to swim' — though both exist). Applies to: like, love, hate, enjoy. Hobbies often use this: 'I love reading,' 'She enjoys cooking,' 'He hates exercise' (NOT 'exercising' possible but less natural). Questions: 'Do you like...ing?' Questions about hobbies are very common in English for getting to know people. Spanish uses infinitive after 'gustar,' but English requires -ing for these verbs.

practice.answer: swimming
Do you like dancing?
She doesn't like cooking

practice.prompt: List 3 hobbies: what you like, love, hate
practice.answer: I like swimming. I love reading. I hate cooking.

practice.prompt: I enjoy watching TV in the evening
practice.answer: I enjoy watching TV in the evening

days.grammar: Forming comparatives: -er/more; irregular forms; comparison structures
days.notes: Comparative adjectives are used to compare two things. Formation depends on adjective length: (1) SHORT adjectives (one syllable, sometimes two): add -er: big → bigger, small → smaller, tall → taller, old → older, new → newer, nice → nicer, long → longer, short → shorter, easy → easier. (2) LONG adjectives (two syllables ending in -y, or three+ syllables): use 'more' + adjective: beautiful → more beautiful, expensive → more expensive, interesting → more interesting, comfortable → more comfortable. (3) Spelling rules for -er: adjectives ending in -e add just -r (nice → nicer), adjectives ending in consonant + -y change -y to -ier (happy → happier), adjectives ending in consonant + vowel + consonant double the consonant (big → bigger). (4) IRREGULAR comparatives: good → better, bad → worse, far → further/farther. Structure for comparison: subject + verb + comparative + than + object. Examples: 'She is taller than me,' 'This car is more expensive than that one,' 'English is easier than Chinese,' 'The weather today is better than yesterday.' Spanish speakers should note that Spanish uses 'más + adjective + que' for most comparatives, while English has -er for short adjectives and 'more' for long ones. English also allows 'She is taller than I am' (formal) or 'She is taller than me' (casual). The casual form is more natural at A1 level.

examples.en: She is taller than her brother.
examples.es: Ella es más alta que su hermano.

examples.en: This restaurant is more expensive than that one.
examples.es: Este restaurante es más caro que ese.

examples.en: English is easier than German.
examples.es: El inglés es más fácil que el alemán.

examples.en: My house is bigger than yours.
examples.es: Mi casa es más grande que la tuya.

examples.en: The weather today is better than yesterday.
examples.es: El clima hoy es mejor que ayer.

examples.en: She is younger than me, but she is smarter than me!
examples.es: Ella es más joven que yo, ¡pero ella es más inteligente que yo!

days.tip: SHORT adjectives add -ER: tall→taller, big→bigger, old→older. LONG adjectives use MORE: beautiful→more beautiful, expensive→more expensive. IRREGULAR: good→better, bad→worse. Structure: Subject + be + comparative + than + object. 'She is taller than him.' Spelling: -e→-er (nice→nicer), -y→-ier (happy→happier), double consonant (big→bigger). Spanish 'más + adj + que' = English -er/more + adj + than.

practice.answer: taller than
more beautiful, newer, more interesting, smaller
My city is more beautiful than Paris (or: less beautiful than)

practice.prompt: This film is ___ (good) than that one
practice.answer: better

practice.prompt: Compare: you vs friend, your house vs neighbor's, English vs another language
practice.answer: I am taller than my friend. My house is bigger than theirs. English is easier than Chinese.

days.grammar: Forming superlatives: -est/most; irregular forms; superlative structure
days.notes: Superlative adjectives describe the MOST or LEAST of something in a group. Formation: (1) SHORT adjectives: the + adjective + -est: the tallest, the biggest, the fastest, the oldest, the newest, the nicest. (2) LONG adjectives: the most + adjective: the most beautiful, the most expensive, the most interesting, the most comfortable. (3) IRREGULAR: good → the best, bad → the worst, far → the furthest. Spelling rules for -est: adjectives ending in -e add just -st (nice → the nicest), adjectives ending in consonant + -y change -y to -iest (happy → the happiest), adjectives ending in consonant + vowel + consonant double the consonant (big → the biggest). Structure: the + superlative + (in/of the group). Examples: 'She is the tallest in her class,' 'This is the most expensive restaurant in the city,' 'He is the best player on the team,' 'It was the worst day of my life.' Note that you use 'in' for places and 'of' for groups. Spanish speakers use 'el/la/los/las más + adjective' which is similar to English 'the most + adjective.' The key difference is that English REQUIRES 'the' before the superlative, while Spanish sometimes omits it. Also, superlatives are less common in casual conversation than comparatives, so focus on practice with comparatives first.

examples.en: She is the tallest person in her family.
examples.es: Ella es la persona más alta de su familia.

examples.en: This is the most beautiful city I've ever seen.
examples.es: Esta es la ciudad más hermosa que he visto.

examples.en: He is the best player on the team.
examples.es: Él es el mejor jugador del equipo.

examples.en: That was the worst experience of my life.
examples.es: Esa fue la peor experiencia de mi vida.

examples.en: The Eiffel Tower is the most famous monument in Paris.
examples.es: La Torre Eiffel es el monumento más famoso de París.

examples.en: Monday was the coldest day of the week.
examples.es: El lunes fue el día más frío de la semana.

days.tip: SUPERLATIVES = THE MOST/LEAST: Short adjectives: the + adj + -est (the tallest). Long adjectives: the most + adj (the most beautiful). IRREGULAR: good→best, bad→worst. ALWAYS use 'the': 'the tallest' (NOT 'tallest' alone). Use 'in' for places (the best in London), 'of' for groups (the best of the students). Spelling: -e→-st (nicest), -y→-iest (happiest), double consonant (biggest).

practice.answer: the biggest
the most beautiful, the newest, the most interesting, the tallest
This restaurant is the most expensive in the city
the worst

practice.prompt: Describe: tallest person you know, best film, most interesting subject
practice.answer: My brother is the tallest in my family. That film is the best I've seen. English is the most interesting subject.

flashcards.front: She is the tallest in her class

days.grammar: Going to, like + -ing, comparatives, superlatives
days.notes: Week 9 has expanded your range into the future and into expressing preferences and comparisons. 'Going to' lets you talk about plans and near-future actions, which is essential for making arrangements and discussing intentions. Like/love/hate/enjoy + -ing lets you talk about hobbies and what you enjoy doing — crucial for making friends and small talk. Comparatives and superlatives let you describe differences between things and identify the best or worst of something. Combined, these give you the ability to: plan future activities ('I'm going to travel next month'), talk about your interests ('I love reading'), compare things ('This is bigger than that'), and identify extremes ('This is the best'). By mastering Week 9, you can have more complex conversations about yourself, your plans, and your opinions. You're building toward natural, fluent English. Week 10 brings you to A1 graduation with a comprehensive review and introduction to false friends/common mistakes for Spanish speakers.

examples.en: What are your plans? I'm going to study next year. I love reading, so I'm going to read more. I also enjoy traveling. I prefer visiting quiet places. I think mountains are more beautiful than beaches. The most beautiful place I've ever seen is Switzerland!
examples.es: ¿Cuáles son tus planes? Voy a estudiar el próximo año. Me encanta leer, así que voy a leer más. También disfruto viajar. Prefiero visitar lugares tranquilos. Creo que las montañas son más hermosas que las playas. ¡El lugar más hermoso que he visto es Suiza!

examples.en: I'm going to start a new hobby. I'm not sure what yet. I like playing sports, so I'm going to try tennis. I hate swimming, but I enjoy dancing. Dancing is more fun than sports, I think. The most important thing is that I enjoy the activity!
examples.es: Voy a empezar una nuevo pasatiempo. No estoy seguro cuál. Me gusta jugar deportes, así que voy a intentar tenis. Odio nadar, pero disfruto bailar. Bailar es más divertido que los deportes, creo. ¡Lo más importante es que disfrute la actividad!

examples.en: Which is better: your house or theirs? My house is older, but theirs is bigger. Their garden is more beautiful than mine. But I like my house because it's quieter. I'm going to redecorate next summer. I'm going to paint the walls and buy new furniture. I love decorating! It's the most fun activity for me.
examples.es: ¿Cuál es mejor: tu casa o la de ellos? Mi casa es más vieja, pero la de ellos es más grande. Su jardín es más hermoso que el mío. Pero me gusta mi casa porque es más tranquila. Voy a redecor el próximo verano. Voy a pintar las paredes y comprar muebles nuevos. ¡Me encanta decorar! ¡Es la actividad más divertida para mí!

days.tip: WEEK 9 SUMMARY: (1) 'Going to' = plans/near future (I'm going to travel). (2) Like/love/hate/enjoy + -ing for preferences (I like reading). (3) Comparative: subject + be + -er/more + than (taller than). (4) Superlative: the + -est/most (the tallest). Combine all: 'I'm going to visit the most beautiful city, which is bigger than my town, and I'm going to enjoy every minute!'

practice.instruction: Describe future plans and current interests
practice.prompt: Write 6-8 sentences about: going to + plan, like + hobby, comparison, superlative
practice.answer: I'm going to travel next summer. I love traveling and I enjoy exploring new places. My favorite destination is Paris, which is more beautiful than London in my opinion. The best time to visit is spring. I'm going to visit the most famous museums. I like painting, and Paris has the most amazing art collections!

practice.prompt: Compare 3 things, identify which is best/worst, say why you prefer one
practice.answer: Tennis is more fun than swimming. Football is the most exciting sport for me. I prefer tennis because it's easier than football. I hate swimming because the water is cold. I'm going to join a tennis club next month!

practice.prompt: List 5 hobbies: what you like/love/hate/enjoy, rank them, compare them
practice.answer: I love reading. I like swimming. I enjoy dancing. I hate exercising at the gym. Reading is more relaxing than swimming. Dancing is the most fun. I'm going to spend more time reading because it's the best hobby for me.

practice.instruction: Future plans with preferences and comparisons
practice.prompt: Describe a perfect day in future: what you'll do, what you like, how things compare
practice.answer: Tomorrow is going to be perfect. I'm going to wake up early. I love mornings because they're more peaceful than evenings. I'm going to have breakfast with my family. Then I'm going to go hiking. Mountains are more beautiful than parks. I enjoy hiking more than any other activity. It will be the best day!

practice.prompt: Write: going to + comparative + superlative + preference
practice.answer: I'm going to study two languages next year. English is easier than Spanish. Chinese is the most difficult language. But I enjoy studying Chinese more than English because it's the most interesting challenge. It will be an amazing experience!

flashcards.back: Short adj + -er + than / long adj: more + adj + than
the + short adj + -est / the + most + long adj

flashcards.front: Combine: 'I'm going to travel to the most beautiful city, which is more interesting than my town.'

quiz.q: Complex sentence: 'I ___ (going to) enjoy the ___ (beautiful) city in ___'
options: am going to, most beautiful, my country
going to, more beautiful, my country
I like swimming and I'm going to travel to the most beautiful beach, which is bigger than my town

quiz.q: Correct comparison/superlative: 'This is ___ (good) than that and ___ (good) I've seen'

options: loves, dancing, am going to, dance
love, dance, am going to, dance
love, dancing, am going to, dance
the most interesting, it's more complex than others
most interesting, it's the more complex
interesting, it's the most complex one

days.grammar: Transport vocabulary; how questions with 'by'; travel expressions
days.notes: Transport vocabulary is essential for daily life and travel. Modes of transport: bus, train, plane, car, bicycle, taxi, boat, metro, tram, motorcycle, scooter, on foot (walking). Related vocabulary: ticket, platform, station, airport, departure, arrival, gate, seat, luggage, boarding pass, delay, on time, canceled. How to ask: 'How do you get to work?' 'How did you get here?' Responses use 'by': 'I go by bus,' 'I go by car,' 'I go by train,' 'I go on foot' (or 'I walk'). Note: 'by bus' (general mode), but 'by plane' (not 'by air'), 'by car' (not 'by automobile'), 'on foot' (not 'by foot' usually). For taxis and specific vehicles: 'I came by taxi,' 'I came by car.' Journey vocabulary: 'How long is the journey?' 'It takes 30 minutes,' 'The trip was long,' 'The flight was delayed,' 'I missed my train.' Common questions: 'What time does the train leave?' 'Where is the bus stop?' 'How much is the ticket?' 'Is this seat taken?' Combining time and transport: 'I usually go to work by bus,' 'I'm going to travel by train next month,' 'The flight took 10 hours.' Travel is a practical and frequently used topic, so mastery here pays dividends for real-world English.

examples.en: How do you get to work? I go by bus.
examples.es: ¿Cómo llegas al trabajo? Voy en autobús.

examples.en: I'm going to travel to Barcelona by plane next month.
examples.es: Voy a viajar a Barcelona en avión el próximo mes.

examples.en: How long is the journey? It takes about 2 hours by train.
examples.es: ¿Cuánto dura el viaje? Toma alrededor de 2 horas en tren.

examples.en: I prefer traveling by car because it's more comfortable than the bus.
examples.es: Prefiero viajar en coche porque es más cómodo que el autobús.

examples.en: The flight was delayed. We arrived 3 hours late.
examples.es: El vuelo se retrasó. Llegamos 3 horas tarde.

examples.en: Do you usually go by taxi? No, I usually go on foot or by bicycle.
examples.es: ¿Normalmente vas en taxi? No, normalmente voy a pie o en bicicleta.

days.tip: TRANSPORT: 'by bus/train/plane/car/taxi,' 'on foot,' or 'walk.' Question: 'How do you get to...?' Answer: 'by + transport.' Common phrase: 'It takes X minutes/hours by [transport].' Journey = trip/voyage (informal). Travel nouns: ticket, station, platform. Present/future: 'I go by bus' (habitual) or 'I'm going to travel by plane' (future plan). These are practical, immediately useful expressions.

practice.prompt: I go ___ bus / I walk ___ foot / I travel ___ plane
practice.answer: by; on; by

practice.prompt: How do you get to the airport?
practice.answer: I go by taxi / I go by car / I take a taxi
takes (or is)
The bus is cheaper than the car, but the car is faster than the bus. The car is more comfortable.

practice.prompt: I'm going to travel to Madrid ___
practice.answer: by train / by plane / by car (choose one)

flashcards.front: How do you get to work?
by train, by plane, by car
I'm going to travel by plane
The train is faster than the bus

quiz.q: Correct response to 'How long is the journey?'
Preference: Why choose car over bus?

days.grammar: Clothing vocabulary; materials; size and fit
days.notes: Clothing vocabulary: shirt, T-shirt, trousers/pants, dress, skirt, shoes, jacket, coat, hat, jeans, suit, trainers/sneakers, socks, underwear, sweater/jumper, blouse, shorts, belt, gloves, scarf, boots. Additional: uniform, pajamas. Materials: cotton, wool, silk, leather, polyester, plastic, metal, rubber, wood, paper, glass, ceramic. Shopping for clothes: 'What size is this?' 'Do you have a smaller/larger size?' 'Can I try this on?' 'How much is this?' 'It fits perfectly,' 'It's too big/small,' 'It's the right size,' 'I like the color,' 'The material is soft.' Describing clothes: 'I'm wearing a blue dress,' 'She has a red coat,' 'He's in a gray suit.' Color adjectives come before the noun: 'black shoes,' 'white shirt,' 'dark blue jeans.' Intensifiers for quality: 'very comfortable,' 'really nice,' 'extremely soft,' 'quite pretty.' Combining: 'I'm going to buy a new cotton shirt,' 'She loves her red leather jacket,' 'This wool sweater is the most comfortable.' This vocabulary connects to A1 because it's practical for daily life, shopping, and describing people. Clothes are also a topic for describing daily activities: 'I wear jeans on weekends,' 'I get dressed in the morning.'

examples.en: I'm going to buy a new cotton shirt.
examples.es: Voy a comprar una nueva camisa de algodón.

examples.en: She is wearing a blue dress. It fits her perfectly.
examples.es: Ella está usando un vestido azul. Le queda perfectamente.

examples.en: Do you have this in a larger size? This one is too small.
examples.es: ¿Tienes esto en una talla más grande? Este es muy pequeño.

examples.en: I like your leather jacket! It's very nice. The material is soft.
examples.es: ¡Me gusta tu chaqueta de cuero! Es muy bonita. El material es suave.

examples.en: What are you wearing today? I'm in a gray suit and black shoes.
examples.es: ¿Qué estás usando hoy? Estoy en un traje gris y zapatos negros.

examples.en: This wool sweater is more comfortable than the cotton one, and it's the warmest material.
examples.es: Este suéter de lana es más cómodo que el de algodón, y es el material más cálido.

days.tip: CLOTHING: Item + color + material. 'Blue cotton shirt' (NOT 'shirt blue cotton'). Fit: 'It's too big/small,' 'It fits perfectly,' 'Do you have my size?' Shopping: 'Can I try this on?' 'How much is it?' Materials matter: 'It's leather,' 'It's wool,' 'It's cotton.' Wearing: 'I'm wearing jeans,' 'She's in a dress.' These are practical, everyday vocabulary.

practice.answer: I'm wearing a blue shirt and black jeans. My shirt is cotton and my jeans are denim. I'm wearing black shoes.

practice.prompt: Ask for size, try on, comment on fit
practice.answer: Do you have this in a medium? Can I try this on? It fits perfectly! How much is it?
The wool sweater is warmer than the cotton one. The cotton sweater is more comfortable. The wool is the warmest material.
He's wearing a gray suit and a white shirt. He's in black shoes. The suit is leather. It's very nice.
I'm going to buy a new wool coat. I prefer wool because it's the most comfortable material.

flashcards.front: shirt, dress, jeans, shoes, jacket, coat
It fits / It's too big / too small
I'm going to buy a new dress
This is the most comfortable sweater

days.grammar: Narrative past tense; connecting words (and, but, because); past + present activities
days.notes: Describing a holiday/trip uses past simple (what happened) often combined with present simple (general facts about the place) and can include present continuous (what's happening now on a trip). Typical holiday narrative: location, time, who, what you did, weather, impressions, food, people, highlights. Connecting words to make narratives flow: 'and' (adding information), 'but' (contrast), 'because' (reason), 'also' (additional), 'first,' 'then,' 'after,' 'finally' (sequence). Example: 'We went to Spain. The weather was beautiful and the people were friendly. We visited Madrid first, and then we went to Barcelona. We saw many museums, but we also went to the beach. Barcelona was the most beautiful city because it's on the coast. We had delicious food and we loved the culture. Finally, we came home after two weeks.' Advanced connecting: 'I went there because I love the weather,' 'I liked the city, but the hotels were expensive,' 'We visited museums and walked in parks.' Vocabulary for trip description: weather (sunny, rainy, cold, hot), people (friendly, rude, helpful), food (delicious, fresh, authentic), attractions (museums, beaches, temples), activities (hiking, swimming, sightseeing, shopping), impressions (amazing, beautiful, disappointing, fantastic, boring). This day consolidates past simple in a practical, extended narrative context that's common in real English.

examples.en: I went to Italy last year. It was wonderful! The weather was sunny and hot. I went to Rome first, and then I went to Venice. Rome was beautiful because it has amazing history. The museums were incredible, but it was very crowded. The food was delicious — I ate pasta and pizza every day. The people were friendly and helpful. After two weeks, I came home. It was the best holiday I've ever had!
examples.es: Fui a Italia el año pasado. ¡Fue maravilloso! El clima fue soleado y caluroso. Fui a Roma primero y luego a Venecia. Roma fue hermosa porque tiene una historia increíble. Los museos fueron increíbles, pero estaba muy concurrido. La comida fue deliciosa — comí pasta y pizza todos los días. La gente fue amable y útil. Después de dos semanas, volví a casa. ¡Fue el mejor viaje que he tenido!

examples.en: Last summer we traveled to the beach. We had a fantastic time! The water was cold, but it was still perfect for swimming. We played football and we went for long walks. We also went to restaurants and tried local food. The town wasn't very big, but it was very quiet and peaceful. We met some nice people from other countries. After a week, we had to leave, but we are going to go back next summer because we loved it so much!
examples.es: El verano pasado viajamos a la playa. ¡Lo pasamos fantásticamente! El agua estaba fría, pero aún era perfecta para nadar. Jugamos fútbol e hicimos caminatas largas. También fuimos a restaurantes e intentamos comida local. El pueblo no era muy grande, pero era muy tranquilo y pacífico. Conocimos a gente amable de otros países. Después de una semana, tuvimos que irme, ¡pero vamos a volver el próximo verano porque nos encantó!

days.tip: HOLIDAY NARRATIVE: Past simple for what happened + connecting words + impressions. Structure: Location/time + weather + what you did + impressions + comparison + reason + conclusion. Connecting words make it flow: 'and,' 'but,' 'because,' 'first,' 'then,' 'finally.' Use comparatives/superlatives: 'It was the most beautiful place,' 'The food was better than home.' This narrative style is common in English — learn it well!

practice.instruction: Describe a past holiday in 6-8 sentences
practice.prompt: Where, when, weather, activities, impressions, why was it good/bad
practice.answer: I went to France last summer. The weather was beautiful and sunny. I visited Paris and saw the Eiffel Tower. I went to museums and walked in parks. The city was the most beautiful I've ever seen. The food was delicious, but it was expensive. The people were friendly. I loved the trip because Paris is amazing. I'm going to go back next year.

practice.prompt: Describe trip with 'and, but, because, first, then, finally'
practice.answer: I went to Spain, and I loved it. The weather was hot, but I enjoyed swimming. I visited Madrid first, and then Barcelona. Barcelona was beautiful because it's on the coast. Finally, I went to the beach. It was the best holiday because of the people and food.

practice.prompt: Describe two places you visited, compare them
practice.answer: I went to the mountains and the beach. The beach was more relaxing than the mountains. The mountains were more beautiful, but the beach was warmer. Both were amazing, but the beach was the most fun because I love swimming.

practice.prompt: Write 8-10 sentences about a trip: introduce, describe journey, activities, people, food, weather, conclusion, future plan
practice.answer: Last year I went to Japan. I traveled by plane for 12 hours. When I arrived, I was tired, but I was excited. Tokyo was amazing! The city was very big and modern. I visited temples and took many photos. The people were very kind and helpful. The food was delicious — I ate sushi, ramen, and tempura. The weather was cold in winter, but it was perfect for visiting. I loved Japan because it's so different from my country. I'm going to go back next year!

practice.instruction: Personal narrative combining past and future
practice.prompt: Describe a past trip and your plans to travel again
practice.answer: I went to Thailand two years ago. I had an amazing time! The beaches were beautiful and the weather was hot. I visited temples and tried authentic Thai food. It was the best trip I've ever had. The people were friendly and the prices were cheap. I loved traveling there because everything was perfect. Now I'm going to go back next year with my family. I want to show them the beautiful places I discovered. I'm very excited about the future trip!

flashcards.front: I visited Rome and then Venice (sequence + and)
I'm going to go back (future plan after past)
flashcards.back: Voy a volver (future after trip narrative)

quiz.q: Connecting word: 'I went to Rome ___ then to Venice'
Past narrative: 'The food was ___ but the price was ___'
Which is best for holiday description?
options: I was going to the beach
I went to the beach last summer
I am going to the beach

quiz.q: Superlative in narrative: '___most beautiful place I ___'
Complete: 'I traveled by train ___ it was very comfortable ___'

days.topic: Common Mistakes for Spanish Speakers — False Friends & Register
days.grammar: Confusing word pairs; register levels; Spanish-influenced errors
days.notes: False friends (palabras engañosas) are words that look similar but have different meanings. Common false friends: 'actually' (adverbially = in reality, NOT 'actualmente' = currently), 'library' (biblioteca = a place for books, NOT 'librería' = bookstore), 'embarrassed' (avergonzado, NOT 'embarazada' = pregnant), 'sensible' (prudent/reasonable, NOT 'sensible' = sensitive/aware), 'exit' (salida = a way out, NOT 'éxito' = success), 'fabric' (tela = material, NOT 'fábrica' = factory), 'carpet' (alfombra = floor covering, NOT 'carpeta' = folder), 'attend' (asistir = to go to an event, NOT just 'atender' = to serve/help), 'realize' (darse cuenta = to notice/understand, NOT just 'realizar' = to carry out/make), 'sympathetic' (compassionate, NOT 'simpático' = nice/friendly). Register refers to formality level: formal (business, academic), neutral (everyday), casual (friends, informal). Common Spanish-influenced errors: (1) Forgetting articles: 'I am teacher' (WRONG) vs 'I am a teacher.' (2) Word order in questions: 'You work where?' vs 'Where do you work?' (3) Double negatives: 'I don't have no money' (dialectal but not standard). (4) Verb-subject inversion: 'Speaks English she?' vs 'Does she speak English?' (5) Using present simple when present continuous needed: 'What you do?' vs 'What are you doing?' (6) Confusing 'ser/estar' with 'to be': forgetting that English has ONE 'to be' for both. (7) Adjective-noun order: 'A car red' vs 'A red car.' (8) Over-translating: trying to translate word-for-word from Spanish. At A1 level, this review is crucial because these mistakes are very common and understanding them prevents fossilization (habits that become hard to break).

examples.en: Actually, I wasn't embarrassed. I went to the library yesterday. The fabric of this carpet is soft. I attend the gym three times a week. I realized that success is not easy. She is a sympathetic person.
examples.es: En realidad, no estaba avergonzado. Fui a la biblioteca ayer. La tela de esta alfombra es suave. Asisto al gimnasio tres veces a la semana. Me di cuenta de que el éxito no es fácil. Ella es una persona compasiva.

examples.en: Common mistakes: 'I am teacher' (WRONG - need 'a'). 'You work where?' (WRONG - say 'Where do you work?'). 'This is a car red' (WRONG - say 'This is a red car'). 'I see' vs 'I am seeing' - different meanings! She is sad all the time (permanent) vs She is being sad (temporary/act).
examples.es: Errores comunes: 'Soy profesor' (en inglés necesitas 'a'). 'Trabajas dónde?' (debes decir 'Where do you work?'). 'Esto es un coche rojo' — el adjetivo viene antes. ¡'I see' vs 'I am seeing' son diferentes!

days.tip: FALSE FRIENDS & MISTAKES: (1) 'Actually' ≠ 'actualmente' (actually = in reality). (2) 'Library' ≠ 'librería' (library = book place). (3) 'Embarrassed' ≠ 'embarazada' (embarrassed = ashamed). (4) 'Sensible' ≠ 'sensible' (sensible = reasonable, NOT sensitive). (5) Word order: Questions start with question word ('Where do you work?'), not 'You work where?' (6) Articles: 'I am a teacher' (ALWAYS need article). (7) Adjectives before nouns: 'a red car' (NOT 'a car red'). (8) Register: Formal ('May I assist you?') vs Casual ('Can I help?'). These corrections now prevent problems later!

practice.answer: Actually = in reality, at present, in fact (not 'currently')
She attends the university (or She is attending)
I am a teacher (need the article 'a')

practice.prompt: Is this a library or a bookstore? (biblioteca or librería?)
practice.es: It's a library (for books to borrow) vs a bookstore (to buy books)

practice.prompt: How would you ask for help formally vs casually?
practice.answer: Formal: 'May I ask for assistance?' / Casual: 'Can you help me?' or 'Can I get some help?'

days.grammar: All A1 grammar reviewed; vocabulary marathon; self-evaluation
days.notes: Congratulations! You've completed the A1 curriculum. This final day is a comprehensive review consolidating everything you've learned: Weeks 1-2: Greetings, pronouns, 'to be,' articles, demonstratives, possessives, numbers. Weeks 3-4: Family, appearance, have got, present simple, adverbs of frequency, time. Week 5: Questions, there is/are, places, prepositions, directions, imperatives. Week 6: Food, countable/uncountable, quantifiers, shopping, restaurants. Week 7: Can/can't, imperatives, present continuous. Week 8: Past simple (all forms). Week 9: Going to, preferences + -ing, comparatives, superlatives. Week 10: Travel, clothes, holiday narratives, false friends. By now you can: Introduce yourself with personal information (name, age, origin, family). Describe people and things (appearance, possession, location, qualities). Ask and answer questions about habitual actions, current actions, and past events. Express preferences and opinions. Make plans for the future. Navigate social situations (greetings, politeness, shopping, restaurants). Navigate physical environments (directions, places). Tell stories about your past and plans for your future. At A1 level, you have a FOUNDATION. You can communicate basic needs and simple ideas. You're not yet at conversational fluency (that's A2+), but you have the essential grammar and vocabulary. Continue practicing by: Reading simple English books, watching English TV with subtitles, having conversations with language partners, writing journal entries in English, listening to English podcasts, and reviewing this curriculum regularly. The most important thing now is CONSISTENCY and EXPOSURE to English. Use it every day, even for 15-30 minutes. Language learning is a marathon, not a sprint. At A1, you've run the first lap successfully!

examples.en: Hello! My name is María. I'm 28 years old and I'm from Spain. I have a family: my parents, one sister, and one brother. My sister is a teacher and she has blonde hair. My family lives in Barcelona, which is a beautiful city on the coast. I love traveling and I'm going to visit France next month. I enjoy reading and playing tennis. I can speak English and Spanish. My brother can't cook, but he can play football. What do you like doing?
examples.es: ¡Hola! Me llamo María. Tengo 28 años y soy de España. Tengo familia: mis padres, una hermana y un hermano. Mi hermana es maestra y tiene cabello rubio. Mi familia vive en Barcelona, que es una ciudad hermosa en la costa. Me encanta viajar y voy a visitar Francia el próximo mes. Disfruto leyendo y jugando tenis. Puedo hablar inglés y español. Mi hermano no puede cocinar, pero puede jugar fútbol. ¿Qué te gusta hacer?

examples.en: Yesterday was amazing! I went to the beach with my friends. The weather was sunny and hot. We played football and we went swimming. The water was cold but beautiful. We had lunch at a restaurant and ate delicious fish. There are many good restaurants near the beach. After lunch, we went for a walk and took photos. We talked about our plans for next summer. I loved the day because the weather was perfect and my friends were very happy. It was the best day of the week!
examples.es: ¡Ayer fue increíble! Fui a la playa con mis amigos. El clima fue soleado y caluroso. Jugamos fútbol y nadamos. El agua estaba fría pero hermosa. Almorzamos en un restaurante y comimos pescado delicioso. Hay muchos buenos restaurantes cerca de la playa. Después del almuerzo, salimos a caminar y tomamos fotos. Hablamos sobre nuestros planes para el próximo verano. Amé el día porque el clima fue perfecto y mis amigos fueron muy felices. ¡Fue el mejor día de la semana!

days.tip: A1 COMPLETION CHECKLIST: You can now (✓ if true): Introduce yourself with personal info, Use all present simple forms (affirmative/negative/questions), Describe people/things with adjectives, Ask and answer 'Where?' 'When?' 'How many?' questions, Use past simple (regular + irregular verbs), Use 'going to' for future plans, Use 'can' for abilities, Express preferences with like/love/hate + -ing, Compare things (comparative/superlative), Understand and use imperatives, Navigate restaurants and shops, Give and follow directions. If all true, you're A1! If some areas are weak, review those weeks before moving to A2.
